topic: Retrieval augmented generation for reducing hallucinations in large language model outputs
final_review:
The integration of retrieval-augmented generation in large language models has been identified as a potential solution to mitigate hallucinations in their outputs (Towhidul Islam Tonmoy et al., 2024; Wan Zhang et al., 2025). Hallucinations in large language models refer to the generation of content that is not based on any actual input or context (Towhidul Islam Tonmoy et al., 2024). Retrieval-augmented generation involves the use of external knowledge sources to inform the generation process, reducing the likelihood of hallucinations (Kurt Shuster et al., 2021). This approach has been explored in various applications, including educational settings (Zongxi Li et al., 2025) and medical education (Josip Vrdoljak et al., 2025; Aidan Gilson et al., 2023).

The use of retrieval-augmented generation in medical education has shown promise in reducing hallucinations and improving the accuracy of generated content (Harsha Nori et al., 2023). However, concerns have been raised regarding the potential limitations and biases of large language models in medical applications (Malik Sallam, 2023; Alowais et al., 2023). In contrast, the use of retrieval-augmented generation in educational settings has been found to be effective in reducing hallucinations and improving student outcomes (Zongxi Li et al., 2025).

The technical capabilities of large language models have also been explored in various studies (OpenAI et al., 2023; Harsha Nori et al., 2023). These studies have highlighted the potential of large language models to generate high-quality content, but also noted the importance of addressing hallucinations and biases (Wan Zhang et al., 2025). Adaptive control of retrieval-augmented generation has been proposed as a potential solution to mitigate hallucinations (Chengyuan Yao et al., 2024).

Despite the progress made in retrieval-augmented generation, there are still significant research gaps in this area (Towhidul Islam Tonmoy et al., 2024). Further studies are needed to fully explore the potential of retrieval-augmented generation in reducing hallucinations and improving the accuracy of large language models (Wan Zhang et al., 2025). Additionally, the application of retrieval-augmented generation in other domains has shown promise (Muhammad Mudassar Yamin et al., 2024).

In conclusion, the integration of retrieval-augmented generation in large language models has been identified as a potential solution to mitigate hallucinations in their outputs. Further research is needed to fully explore the potential of this approach and address the remaining research gaps (Towhidul Islam Tonmoy et al., 2024; Wan Zhang et al., 2025).
